current_fact.fact_text: On 18 March 1766, amid rising colonial unrest, the British Parliament repealed the Stamp Act, a tax on printed materials in the American colonies. Celebrated with bonfires and bells, the repeal seemed like a victory—but it was paired with a Declaratory Act insisting Britain still had full authority. The moment captured a brief exhale before the American Revolution accelerated.

episode.title: Morning Drops #39 - Moving Meditation
episode.description: An episode inspired by how travel helps us see more clearly. Stepping into new places opens the mind, shifts perspective. The music moves through balearic warmth, boogie grooves, world and latin-folk influences, and touches of new wave — a selection built to follow the quiet changes that happen when you're out of your usual rhythm.
songs: España Circo Este - Dormo poco e sogno molto
Talking Heads - This Must Be the Place (Naive Melody)

current_fact.match_reason: A political retreat that briefly eased tensions in the colonies fits an episode about perspective shifts and quiet transitions—both reflect a pause before bigger change arrives.

queue.fact_text: In 1741, a fire destroyed parts of Governor George Clarke’s complex at Fort George in New York, helping ignite what became known as the New York Conspiracy of 1741. Authorities claimed that enslaved Africans and poor whites were plotting uprisings and arson across the city. Fueled by fear and rumor, the investigations led to mass arrests, executions, and deportations whose factual basis remains deeply contested.

episode.title: Morning Drops #34 - Soul Sisters
episode.description: A tribute to four iconic voices — women who have shaped the way we listen, feel, and move. Each track carries a different kind of presence: one that holds you, one that questions, one that grounds, and one that lets go. An energy that is soulful, raw, and unapologetically feminine.
songs: Grace Jones - Walking in the Rain
Aretha Franklin - A Rose Is Still a Rose

queue.match_reason: A panic-tinged episode of colonial injustice against enslaved people connects with a show honoring powerful, questioning voices—artists whose music often confronts racism and structural power.

queue.fact_text: On 18 March 1644, the Third Anglo-Powhatan War began in Virginia when Powhatan warriors launched a coordinated surprise attack on English settlements. The conflict, sparked by decades of encroachment and shifting alliances, was among the last large-scale efforts by the Powhatan to push colonists back. Its end marked a grim turning point for Indigenous power in the region.

episode.title: Morning Drops #16 - The Sound of Tuareg
episode.description: A journey into the sound of the Tuareg: from traditional chants and desert rituals to the electrified rhythms of exile and resistance. Music shaped by sand, memory, and survival.

queue.match_reason: An Indigenous nation’s struggle against colonization resonates with Tuareg desert blues about land, exile, and resistance—two distant but parallel stories of native peoples defending their worlds.

queue.fact_text: On 18 March 1848, an uprising in Milan drove Austrian troops under Marshal Radetzky out of the city after five days of intense street fighting. Citizens improvised barricades from furniture and paving stones, turning a fashionable city into a labyrinth of popular revolt. The victory became a key moment in the broader Revolutions of 1848 and the dream of a unified Italy.

episode.title: Morning Drops #46 - Fog and Fire
episode.description: In today’s Morning Drops, Gaia brings us on a passport-free trip between two Italian energies: the foggy stillness of the North and the volcanic pulse of the South. Throughout the journey, Gaia shares four of her favourite Italian songwriters. Grab your coffee and tune into the radio show from another planet!
songs: Edoardo Bennato - La Torre Di Babele
Lucio Dalla - Com'è Profondo il Mare

queue.match_reason: An Italian city in revolutionary flames pairs with an episode about Italy’s contrasting moods—fog and fire—through its songwriters, tying historic upheaval to modern Italian voice and emotion.

queue.fact_text: In 1899, astronomer William Henry Pickering announced the discovery of Saturn’s moon Phoebe using photographic plates—one of the earliest major solar-system finds made from photographs rather than direct observation. The technique allowed astronomers to "rewind" the sky by re-examining plates, spotting faint moving dots they’d missed before. It marked a quiet revolution in how we see and catalogue the cosmos.

episode.title: Morning Drops #47 - The Rise Of Autotune
episode.description:
In this Morning Drop, Yasmin explores the explosive rise of Auto-Tune, a tool that quietly changed the sound of music without asking for attention. From fragile, imperfect recordings to voices shaped with precision, this episode follows the moment when correction became standard and sound became controlled.

Along the way, we discover the curious story of how it was invented, how it moved from the background of studios to the center of debate, and how a few artists turned it into something expressive rather than hidden.

Listen slowly, and let us know what you think about Autotune.
songs: Chet Baker – I Fall in Love Too Easily

queue.match_reason: A new photographic technique quietly reshaping astronomy pairs with Auto-Tune quietly reshaping music—both are tools that changed how humans perceive, capture, and refine signals from the world.

queue.fact_text: On 18 March 1965, Alexei Leonov’s historic spacewalk almost ended in disaster when his spacesuit ballooned in the vacuum, making him too large to fit back through the Voskhod 2 airlock. To survive, he manually bled pressure from his suit, risking decompression sickness, and re-entered headfirst against procedure. He later admitted his heart was racing so fast that his spacesuit’s sensors couldn’t keep up.

episode.title: Morning Drops #42 - The Joy Of Learning
episode.description:
Hosted by Yasmin, this Morning Drop explores learning as a lifelong process, focusing on persistence, repetition, and the emotional landscape that comes with challenging oneself over time.

Through personal stories and scientific insights, the episode looks at how learning reshapes the brain, generates joy through effort, and why progress often arrives after confusion and frustration. It reflects on the so-called delayed pleasure effect, the role of dopamine in motivation, and how curiosity and learning support long-term wellbeing.

The episode also questions our cultural relationship with failure and invites a softer, more realistic approach to learning, one that accepts mistakes as part of growth and sees persistence as a quiet form of strength.

Mood: reflective, grounded, quietly hopeful
Music genre: alternative rock, hip hop, electronic, neo-soul, drill

A space to listen slowly and stay with the process.

queue.match_reason: Leonov’s near-fatal improvisation in orbit reflects an extreme form of trial, error, and adaptation—themes mirrored in an episode about learning through confusion, failure, and persistence.

queue.fact_text: In 1848, the revolutionary wave sweeping Europe reached Milan, where civilians forced Austrian troops out in a dramatic five-day uprising. While short-lived, the victory electrified other movements and showed how quickly a city’s everyday rhythms could transform into a collective push for change. The echoes of those barricades would later shape Italy’s path toward unification.

episode.title: Morning Drops #17 - Steel City
episode.description: Gaia takes us on a short trip through Sheffield's sounds — from electronic textures to raw guitars and sharp storytelling. Four tracks, one city, different edges of the same story.
songs: The Human League - The Sound Of The Crowd

queue.match_reason: A city’s identity being re-forged through revolt is paired with an episode about another city whose soundtracks capture working-class struggle and reinvention—Milan’s barricades meeting Sheffield’s guitars.

published.fact_text: On 18 March 1965, Soviet cosmonaut Alexei Leonov left his spacecraft Voskhod 2 and became the first human to walk in space. His 12-minute spacewalk was so risky that his suit ballooned, he barely fit back through the hatch, and he had to vent air from the suit to survive. The mission was kept partially secret for years because of how close it came to disaster.

episode.title: Morning Drops #03 - Space Shift
episode.description: Gaia invites you to shift your mindset with a simple yet powerful reflection: What if we saw emptiness not as a void but as space to create? An energising and feel-good selection of tracks, the perfect way to ease into your day and set the tone for the week ahead.
songs: Willy J Healey - Woke Up Smiling
The Strokes - You Only Live Once

published.match_reason: A literal first step into outer space pairs with an episode about reimagining emptiness as creative space, both celebrating bold leaps into the unknown.

published.fact_text: In 1899, astronomers announced the discovery of Phoebe, an irregular, dark moon of Saturn first identified on photographic plates taken in 1898. Unlike Saturn’s bright inner moons, Phoebe orbits backwards and far out, suggesting it may be a captured object from the outer Solar System. It would not be seen up close until the Cassini spacecraft flew past it a century later.

episode.title: Evening Flows #01 - Yasmin - Crescendo in the Medina
episode.description: The first Evening Flow episode comes from Yasmin. It starts deep in the medina, slow, mysterious, textured, and grows into a liberating dance across sub-Saharan landscapes. A journey through ambient soundscapes, Arabic melodies, Afro-Latin rhythms, and funky highlife grooves.
songs: David Visan & Carlos Campos - Crystal Heart
Evan Mars - Ya Zareef Al Tool
Dele Sosimi & Medlar - You No Fit Touch Am (Medlar Remix)

published.match_reason: A mysterious, distant Saturnian moon discovered on old plates fits the slow, cosmic ambience and ‘solar wind’ textures of this Evening Flow set.

published.fact_text: On 18 March 1980, a Vostok-2M rocket exploded during fueling at Plesetsk Cosmodrome’s Site 43, killing 48 people. The Soviet Union kept the disaster secret for nearly a decade, and only later did detailed accounts of the accident and its victims emerge. It remains one of the deadliest ground accidents in rocketry history, a stark reminder of the risks behind spaceflight.

episode.title: Morning Drops #45 - It Can't Rain Forever
episode.description:
In this first broadcast of 2026, Gaia returns from the vivid landscapes of Morocco to open a space for reflection. This episode navigates the delicate transition between closing a significant chapter and stepping into the new year. It explores the concept of constancy amidst change, celebrating the grounding force of friendship and the creative sanctuary that is Sedna FM.

The musical selection mirrors this journey, moving from the orchestral depth of changing seasons into the golden warmth of psychedelic soul. It recalls memories of Swiss mountains and unexpected downpours, reminding us that shared optimism can weather any storm.

Take a moment to breathe in the new morning and listen deeply.

Mood: Reflective, Warm, Transitional, Hopeful
Music genre: Classic Rock, Psychedelic Soul, Pop Rock, Soft Rock

published.match_reason: A tragic rocket disaster and its long-hidden grief are paired with a reflective episode about storms, recovery, and the quiet hope that it can’t "rain" forever.

published.fact_text: In 1848, William Henry Fry’s opera *Leonora* premiered in Philadelphia, becoming the first known grand opera by an American composer to be publicly performed. At a time when Americans heavily imported European culture, Fry boldly argued that the United States needed its own large-scale musical works. His experiment planted an early flag for American composers on the operatic stage.

episode.title: Sedna FM #01 - November Bomb
episode.description: The first Family & Friends radio episode from Yasmin Sarbaoui and Gaia Parolini. Where it all began — a life soundtrack that launched Sedna FM into the universe. Raw, heartfelt, and full of the energy that defines everything that followed.
songs: Canserbero - De la vida como pelicula, tragedia, comedia y ficcion

published.match_reason: The first grand opera by an American composer mirrors Sedna FM’s own debut episode: both are bold first chapters staking out a new musical territory.

published.fact_text: On 18 March 1953, the Yenice–Gönen earthquake struck western Turkey with a magnitude of about 7.4, killing over a thousand people. Entire villages were heavily damaged as the North Anatolian Fault released pent-up strain in seconds. The event contributed to better understanding of Turkey’s seismic hazards and the importance of building for the ground’s hidden moods.

episode.title: Morning Drops #14 - Slow Planet
episode.description: A pause. A quiet return to slower rhythms, sparked by a live set under the summer sky. A moment to breathe and settle into the week.

published.match_reason: A violent jolt from the Earth’s crust is contrasted with an episode about slowing down and grounding, offering a gentle sonic antidote to seismic chaos.

published.fact_text: In March 1848, the "Five Days of Milan" uprising saw citizens build barricades and fight Austrian troops street by street, forcing Marshal Radetzky to withdraw from the city. Ordinary residents—workers, students, and intellectuals—turned Milan into a maze of resistance. Their success became a powerful symbol in the wider Italian struggle for independence.

episode.title: Morning Drops #49 - Rage Against The Machine
episode.description:
In this Morning Drop, Yasmin revisits the story of Rage Against the Machine — a band born in tension, shaped by conviction, and remembered for moments that blurred the line between stage and protest.
From early Los Angeles unrest to Wall Street performances, this episode moves through rupture, resistance, and legacy — carried by songs that still feel urgent today.
Listen closely.
songs: Rage Against the Machine - Killing in the Name
Rage Against the Machine - Without a Face
Rage Against the Machine - Guerrilla Radio
Rage Against the Machine - Sleep Now in the Fire

published.match_reason: An urban rebellion that turned streets into battlegrounds pairs naturally with a politically charged episode centered on music-as-protest and resistance.

published.fact_text: In 1068, a series of powerful earthquakes struck the Levant and the Arabian Peninsula, devastating cities from Palestine to Arabia and killing tens of thousands of people. Medieval chroniclers described collapsing walls, ground ruptures, and aftershocks that terrified communities for months. The event is one of the earliest well-documented multi-fault earthquake sequences in the region’s history.

episode.title: Morning Drops #02 - Waves of the Maghreb
episode.description: Oriental vibes guided by Yasmin. Discovering curious facts about the Maghreb, together with its fascinating music! A wonderful trip with a selection of songs from North Africa.
songs: Nass El Ghiwane - El Madi Fate

published.match_reason: A historic Near Eastern quake sequence connects with an episode steeped in North African and Mediterranean soundscapes, both rooted in the same broad region’s history and culture.

published.fact_text: On 18 March 1971 in Peru, a massive landslide plunged into Yanawayin Lake, generating a wave that surged over the mining camp of Chungar and nearby settlements. Around 200 people were killed as water and debris obliterated buildings in seconds. It’s a stark example of how high mountain lakes can transform landslides into unexpected inland tsunamis.

episode.title: Morning Drops #33 - Immerse
episode.description: An analogy between diving, one of Gaia's great passions, and the act of immersing in life itself. A reflection on what happens when we go below the surface: when we let ourselves be fully present. From moments of calm suspension to warm, rhythmic flow, the selection moves like water — fluid, organic, and alive. An invitation to slow down, listen deeper, and float.
songs: Pino D'Angiò - Un Po' d'Uva e un Liquore

published.match_reason: A destructive plunge of earth into a lake is reframed through a water-themed episode about immersion, turning the raw power of waves into a gentler, reflective aquatic journey.

published.fact_text: In AD 37, after the death of Emperor Tiberius, the Roman Senate annulled his will and proclaimed Gaius Julius Caesar Augustus Germanicus—better known as Caligula—emperor. Initially adored as a fresh, generous young ruler, Caligula’s reign soon became infamous for brutality, extravagance, and bizarre displays of power. His nickname "Little Boots" came from the tiny military boots he wore as a child mascot on campaign.

episode.title: Sedna FM #06 - Alters of an Ego
episode.description: What if you could step outside yourself and become someone freer, bolder, or completely unexpected? Exploring the artistry of musicians who craft alter-egos and parallel realities to experiment with new dimensions of their identity. It's not about hiding who they are — it's about expanding who they can be.
songs: The Fireman - Strawberries Oceans Ships Forest

published.match_reason: Caligula’s dramatic shift from beloved heir to notorious ruler echoes the theme of powerful public personas and transformed identities explored through musical alter-egos.

published.fact_text: On 18 March 1793, during the War of the First Coalition, French forces under General Dumouriez were defeated at the Battle of Neerwinden in present-day Belgium. Ironically, Dumouriez had won a different Battle of Neerwinden at the same place just a year earlier. The reversal pushed him toward treason against the French Republic, showing how fast fortunes can flip in revolutionary times.

episode.title: Morning Drops #31 - A Trip-Hop Journey
episode.description: Hosted by friend Lorenzo, a journey through the roots and evolution of trip-hop. From Bristol's early experiments with Massive Attack and Tricky, to DJ Shadow's cinematic sampling in California, and Thievery Corporation's global downtempo grooves in Washington D.C. — exploring how this genre blurred boundaries and reshaped electronic music.
songs: Tricky - Hell Is Round the Corner
DJ Shadow - Midnight in a Perfect World

published.match_reason: A foggy, shifting battlefield in revolutionary Europe pairs with an episode about trip-hop’s blurred edges and cinematic tension—both living in the space between clear winners and losers.

published.fact_text: In 1994, Bosniak and Croat leaders signed the Washington Agreement, ending their bitter conflict within Bosnia and Herzegovina and creating a joint federation. The accord transformed former enemies into uneasy partners to resist larger forces threatening to tear the country apart. It showed how borders can be redrawn not only on maps, but inside people’s identities.

episode.title: Morning Drops #44 - Residente
episode.description:
In this edition of Morning Drops, Yasmin navigates the complex geography of René Pérez Joglar, known to the world as Residente. His story is not just a musical evolution; it is a timeline marked by resistance, lyrical obsession, and a relentless search for identity.

From the gated streets of Trujillo Alto to the global stage, we explore how a restless mind transformed into the voice of a generation. The selection moves through the textures of his life: hearing the raw energy of his early rebellion, the sharp precision of his political critique, and finally, the fragile silence of his most personal confessions.

It is a study in duality. We witness the rapper who dismantles giants and the son who misses his mother. It is a soundscape that proves that sometimes, the loudest revolution happens inside.

Mood: Raw, Revolutionary, Introspective, Honest
Music genre: Latin Alternative, Hip Hop, Urban, World

published.match_reason: A hard-won political peace between communities mirrors an episode about identity, resistance, and reconciliation within one artist and his divided homeland.

published.fact_text: On 18 March 1969, the United States began Operation Menu, a secret bombing campaign against targets along the Sihanouk Trail in neutral Cambodia. For over a year the strikes were concealed even from parts of the U.S. government, logged under code names like "Breakfast" and "Lunch." The campaign helped destabilize Cambodia, with consequences that would echo far beyond official maps.

episode.title: Morning Drops #27 - Nomophobia
episode.description: Yasmin reflects on what happened when she recently lost her phone and why it felt freeing. A dive into the psychology of our relationship with smartphones: from nomophobia (the fear of being without a phone), to digital burnout and cognitive overload, to the surprising benefits of boredom and the brain's default mode network. An invitation to rethink how we connect and disconnect.

published.match_reason: A covert operation hidden behind code names resonates with an episode about the unseen infrastructures—networks, screens, systems—that quietly shape how we live and perceive reality.

published.fact_text: In 1959, the Hawaii Admission Act was signed into law, setting the stage for Hawaii to become the 50th U.S. state. The islands’ path to statehood was shaped by a complex history of indigenous sovereignty, monarchy, annexation, and strategic importance in the Pacific. When Hawaiians later voted for statehood, it formally bound a volcanic archipelago in the middle of the ocean to a continental nation.

episode.title: Morning Drops #21 - Endless Summer
episode.description: Recorded from Fano, a quiet town on the Adriatic coast where summer stretches long and slow. Between swims, simple meals, and time with family, Gaia reflects on the kind of music that helps us return to ourselves. Through different shades of jazz — from minimal and meditative to warm and rhythmic — an invitation to slip into that endless summer feeling.

published.match_reason: A Pacific island chain officially joining a union is paired with a sun-soaked coastal episode, both radiating warm, ocean-side "endless summer" energy with deeper histories beneath the surface.

published.fact_text: On 18 March 1944, Mount Vesuvius erupted for the last time in the 20th century, spewing lava and ash down its slopes. The eruption damaged nearby villages and destroyed dozens of Allied bombers parked at an airfield—one of the rare times a volcano has wiped out an air fleet. Today, millions live in its shadow, making Vesuvius one of the world’s most closely watched volcanoes.

episode.title: Morning Drops #40 - Diving With Orcas
episode.description: Yasmin's friend Caroline takes us beyond the polar circle, into the Arctic waters of Norway, where orcas and humpback whales gather each winter. A personal journey shaped by a quiet obsession with orcas, from Patagonia to the icy fjords of the north. The soundscape moves gently between cinematic swells and soft, rhythmic pulses — echoing the cold light of Arctic mornings and the grace of underwater encounters.

published.match_reason: An iconic Italian volcano’s dramatic eruption meets an episode about awe-inspiring natural forces in another extreme landscape—volcanic fire balanced by Arctic water, both scored cinematically.

published.fact_text: In 1940, Adolf Hitler and Benito Mussolini met at the Brenner Pass in the Alps and agreed to cement their alliance against France and the United Kingdom. The mountaintop summit, held above a Europe already at war, symbolized the tightening of the Axis partnership. The decisions made there would ripple through battlefields, cities, and lives across the continent.

episode.title: Morning Drops #22 - Sicilians On The Dolomites
episode.description: Hosted by Yasmin, a musical journey inspired by the mountains. Joined by two special guests, exploring the tracks they've chosen, hearing the stories behind their picks, and discovering the personal memories each song brings to life — a blend of music, voices, and landscapes you can hear.
songs: Florian Pellissier Quintet - Coup de foudre à Thessalonique
Lucio Dalla & Francesco De Gregori - Ma come fanno i marinai

published.match_reason: A tense wartime meeting in an Alpine pass connects with a mountain-themed episode; both take place high above the plains, but one turns the peaks into a space for music and stories instead of alliances.

published.fact_text: In 1241, Mongol forces invading Poland crushed local armies at the Battle of Chmielnik, opening the way to plunder Kraków. Contemporary accounts describe swift cavalry, feigned retreats, and smoke screens used to confuse defenders—tactics that made the Mongol army feel almost supernatural. The campaign showed how a nomadic empire could project power deep into settled Europe.

episode.title: Morning Drops #20 - Hitchhiking Poland
episode.description: Yasmin takes you back to the summer of 2020, when borders were still uncertain and a long-held wish to visit Poland finally came true. Through four tracks, tracing her journey from a stranger's couch in Poznań to the peaks of Zakopane.

published.match_reason: A medieval Mongol incursion into Poland is mirrored by a modern, peaceful journey across the same land—trading cavalry raids for couches, mountains, and a soundtrack of Polish rock.

published.fact_text: On 18 March 1915, during the Gallipoli campaign of World War I, three Allied battleships were sunk in a single day while attempting to force the Dardanelles Strait. Naval mines, underestimated by planners, ripped open the hulls of modern warships. The failed assault helped turn the campaign into a grinding land battle remembered for its harsh conditions and high casualties.

episode.title: Morning Drops #31 - Sailing With Germans
episode.description: From the deck of a sailing boat, hosted by Yasmin and her special guests. A week at sea with six Germans, each with their own stories, songs, and ways of moving through the waves. From shared meals to unexpected laughter, from quiet resilience to sun-soaked joy — a collection of voices and tracks that reflect what it means to journey together.
songs: Ben Howard - Keep Your Head Up

published.match_reason: A disastrous naval push through the Dardanelles is reimagined alongside a gentle, communal sailing trip—two very different stories of ships, straits, and what can happen at sea.

published.fact_text: On 18 March 1766, amid rising colonial unrest, the British Parliament repealed the Stamp Act, a tax on printed materials in the American colonies. Celebrated with bonfires and bells, the repeal seemed like a victory—but it was paired with a Declaratory Act insisting Britain still had full authority. The moment captured a brief exhale before the American Revolution accelerated.

episode.title: Morning Drops #39 - Moving Meditation
episode.description: An episode inspired by how travel helps us see more clearly. Stepping into new places opens the mind, shifts perspective. The music moves through balearic warmth, boogie grooves, world and latin-folk influences, and touches of new wave — a selection built to follow the quiet changes that happen when you're out of your usual rhythm.
songs: España Circo Este - Dormo poco e sogno molto
Talking Heads - This Must Be the Place (Naive Melody)

published.match_reason: A political retreat that briefly eased tensions in the colonies fits an episode about perspective shifts and quiet transitions—both reflect a pause before bigger change arrives.
